``` ___ _____ _ ____ _ | _ \ |_ _| | | / ___| ___ __| | ___ _ __ | |_) | | | | | | | / _ \ / _` | / _ \ | '__| | _ < | | | |___ | |___ | (_) | | (_| | | __/ | | |_| \_\ |_| |_____| \____| \___/ \__,_| \___| |_| ```

📄 Papers | 📚 Repo-intro | 🔄 RTLCoder-flow | 📊 Dataset-generation | 🧪 Inference | 🏋️ Training | 📝 Citation

## 📄 Papers This is the official implementation for RTLCoder. We have three relevant accepted papers. Please prioritize citing our IEEE TCAD version if you find our repo helpful. **RTLCoder: Fully Open-Source and Efficient LLM-Assisted RTL Code Generation Technique**.[[paper]](https://zhiyaoxie.github.io/files/TCAD25_RTLCoder.pdf) IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems (TCAD) (Early Access). **RTLCoder: Outperforming GPT-3.5 in Design RTL Generation with Our Open-Source Dataset and Lightweight Solution**.[[paper]](https://zhiyaoxie.com/files/LAD24_RTLCoder.pdf) IEEE International Workshop on LLM-Aided Design (LAD), 2024. (Best Paper Nomination ). **OpenLLM-RTL: Open Dataset and Benchmark for LLM-Aided Design RTL Generation** (Invited).[[paper]](https://zhiyaoxie.com/files/ICCAD24_OpenLLM.pdf) IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer-Aided Design (ICCAD), 2024. ## 📚 Repo-intro _**Note**: The model, dataset, inference scripts, data generation flow and training flow are provided._ _**Important**: If you want to test **RTLCoder-Deepseek**, please check our comments within the **benchmark_inference/test_on_verilog-eval.py** file. They are the response postprocessing method designed for **RTLCoder-Deepseek**. The default inference script is for **RTLCoder-Mistral**._ Targeting Verilog code generation, we propose an automated flow to generate a large labeled dataset with diverse Verilog design problems and answers. It addresses the serious data availability challenge in IC design-related tasks, and its potential applications are not limited to LLMs. The LLM directly trained on it can already achieve comparable accuracy with GPT-3.5. We also introduce a new LLM training scheme based on code quality feedback. It further boosts the ultimate model performance to outperform GPT-3.5. And we further revised the training process from an algorithm perspective to reduce its GPU memory consumption. TABLE 1 summarizes existing works in LLM-based design RTL generation. TABLE 1: LLM-based works on design RTL generation (e.g., Verilog). **In our work, we provide four RTL code generation models that are available on the HuggingFace platform.** 1. [RTLCoder-Deepseek-v1.1](https://huggingface.co/ishorn5/RTLCoder-Deepseek-v1.1). This model was finetund on DeepSeek-coder-6.7b. It has the best performance on VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks but with a relatively lower inference speed compared with the following models. The RTLCoder-Deepseek-v1.1 may not stop even when the required output text is finished. So We need to extract the required code part before the keyword"endmodulemodule" from the output sequence and add an "endmodule" at the end. 3. [RTLCoder-v1.1](https://huggingface.co/ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1). (Finetuned based on Mistral-v0.1) 4. [RTLCoder-v1.1-gptq-4bit](https://huggingface.co/ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1-gptq-4bit). (The GPTQ version of RTLCoder-v1.1) 5. [RTLCoder-v1.1-gguf-4bit](https://huggingface.co/ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1-gguf-4bit). This quantized one could run on CPU. (The CPU version of RTLCoder-v1.1) ## 🔄 RTLCoder-flow In this paper, there are two main contributions to obtain the RTLCoder. (1) We first introduce our automated dataset generation flow. It generated our RTL generation dataset with over 27 thousand samples, each sample being a pair of design description instruction and corresponding reference code. We build this automated generation flow by taking full advantage of the powerful general text generation ability of the commercial tool GPT. Please notice that GPT is only used for dataset generation in this work and we adhere to the terms of service of OpenAI, and there is no commercial competition between the proposed RTLcoder and OpenAI's models. The automated dataset generation flow is illustrated in **Figure 1** which includes three stages: 1) RTL domain keywords preparation, 2) instruction generation, and 3) reference code generation. We designed several general prompt templates to control GPT generating the desired outputs in each stage. Figure 1: Our proposed automated dataset generation flow. (2) Besides the new training dataset, we propose a new LLM training scheme that incorporates code quality scoring. It significantly improves the RTLCoder’s performance on the RTL generation task. Also, we revised the training process from the algorithm perspective to reduce the GPU memory consumption of this new training method, allowing implementation with limited hardware resources. The training scheme is illustrated in **Figure 2**. Figure 2: Our proposed training scheme based on RTL quality score. ## 📊 Dataset We provide the generation scripts and data samples in the folder **"data_generation"**. You can design your own prompting method by modifying the file **"p_example.txt"** and **"instruction_gen.py"**. You can expand the existing dataset by running the following command. ``` python instruction_gen.py ``` The 27K instruction-code dataset "Resyn-27k.json" is provided in the "dataset" file. Please kindly note that the dataset was generated by GPT-3.5-turbo and it cannot be guaranteed that all the data are strictly correct. Despite the possible presence of errors in some problem descriptions and design code, we believe that they can still provide valuable information for model training. ## 🧪 Benchmarking (1) Inference demo The input prompt may have a great influence on the generation quality. Ideally, it should describe the circuit "IO" and behavior clearly so that it doesn't contain ambiguity. We provide a template as follows. ``` Please act as a professional verilog designer. Implement a data width conversion circuit that converts 8-bit data input to 16-bit data output. The module provides two output ports: valid_out, which indicates the validity of the output data, and data_out, which represents the converted 16-bit output data. The first arriving 8-bit data should be placed in the higher 8 bits of the 16-bit data output. The valid_out and data_out signals are generated in the next clock cycle after the two data inputs. When there is only one data input, valid_out and data_out are not generated immediately. Instead, they wait for the arrival of the next data input to complete the concatenation of the two data inputs before generating valid_out and data_out. Module name: width_8to16 Input ports: clk: Clock signal used for synchronous operation. rst_n: Active-low reset signal. Defined as 0 for reset and 1 for reset signal inactive. valid_in: Input signal indicating the validity of the input data. data_in: 8-bit input data to be converted. Output ports: valid_out: Output signal indicating the validity of the output data. data_out: 16-bit output data resulting from the width conversion. Implementation: The data_out register is triggered on the positive edge of the clock signal (posedge clk) or the negative edge of the reset signal (negedge rst_n). On reset, indicated by !rst_n, the data_out register is cleared to 0. If the input data is valid (valid_in) and the flag signal is active, the data_out register is updated by concatenating the contents of the data_lock register (8 bits) and the data_in register (8 bits) to form a 16-bit output. The first valid data is temporarily stored, and when the second valid data is inputted, they are concatenated to produce the output valid_out and data_out. Give me the complete code. module width_8to16( input clk , input rst_n , input valid_in , input [7:0] data_in , output reg valid_out, output reg [15:0] data_out ); ``` If you don't have a GPU with more than 4 GB memory, please try the quantized 4-bit version which could run on CPU: [RTLCoder-v1.1-gguf-4bit](https://huggingface.co/ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1-gguf-4bit). Before you try the following code, please install the [ctransformers](https://github.com/marella/ctransformers) package. ``` from ctransformers import AutoModelForCausalLM model_path = 'ggml-model-q4_0.gguf' # Set gpu_layers to the number of layers to offload to GPU. Set to 0 if no GPU acceleration is available on your system. llm = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_path, model_type="mistral", gpu_layers=0, max_new_tokens=2000, context_length=6048, temperature=0.5, top_p=0.95,) prompt = "Please act as a professional verilog designer and provide a half adder. \nmodule half_adder\n(input a, \ninput b, \noutput sum, \n output carry);\n" print(llm(prompt)) ``` For inference using RTLCoder, you can just use the following code. ``` import torch from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM # Prompt prompt = "Please act as a professional verilog designer and provide a half adder. \nmodule half_adder\n(input a, \ninput b, \noutput sum, \n output carry);\n" # Load model and tokenizer # With multiple gpus, you can specify the GPU you want to use as gpu_name (e.g. int(0)). gpu_name = 0 tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("ishorn5/RTLCoder-Deepseek-v1.1") model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("ishorn5/RTLCoder-Deepseek-v1.1", torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map=gpu_name) model.eval() # Sample input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.to(gpu_name) sample = model.generate(input_ids, max_length=512, temperature=0.5, top_p=0.9) s_full = tokenizer.decode(sample[0]) # The RTLCoder-Deepseek-v1.1 may not stop even when the required output text is finished. # We need to extract the required part from the output sequence based on a keyword "endmodulemodule". if len(s_full.split('endmodulemodule', 1)) == 2: s = s_full.split('endmodulemodule', 1)[0] + "\n" + "endmodule" else: s = s_full.rsplit('endmodule', 1)[0] + "\n" + "endmodule" if s.find('top_module') != -1: s = s.split('top_module', 1)[0] s = s.rsplit('endmodule', 1)[0] + "\n" + "endmodule" index = s.rfind('tb_module') if index == -1: index = s.find('testbench') if index != -1: s_tmp = s[:index] s = s_tmp.rsplit("endmodule", 1)[0] + "\n" + "endmodule" print(s) #For "ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1", it will stop generating tokens after completing the coding task. #But you can still use the keyword "endmodule" to extract the code part. #tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1") #model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1", torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map=gpu_name) #model.eval() #Sample #input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.to(gpu_name) #sample = model.generate(input_ids, max_length=512, temperature=0.5, top_p=0.9) #print(tokenizer.decode(sample[0])) ``` To test the RTLCoder-gptq-4bit, you can just use the following code. Before you try the following code, please make sure to have the package [https://github.com/marella/ctransformers](https://github.com/AutoGPTQ/AutoGPTQ). ``` from transformers import AutoTokenizer from [auto_gptq](https://github.com/marella/ctransformers) import AutoGPTQForCausalLM, BaseQuantizeConfig # Prompt prompt = "Please act as a professional verilog designer and provide a half adder. \nmodule half_adder\n(input a, \ninput b, \noutput sum, \n output carry);\n" tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1-gptq-4bit", use_fast=True) model = AutoGPTQForCausalLM.from_quantized("ishorn5/RTLCoder-v1.1-gptq-4bit", device="cuda:0") model.eval() # Sample inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(0) sample = model.generate(**inputs, max_length=512, temperature=0.5, top_p=0.9) print(tokenizer.decode(sample[0])) ``` (2) Test model on Verilog-eval We provide the inference script **"test_on_verilog-eval.py"** for "verilog-eval" benchmark in folder **"benchmark_inference"**. You need to firstly download the "verilog-eval" benchmark. ``` git clone https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval.git ``` Then modify the **"descri_path"** and **"input_path"** in **"test_on_nvbench.py"** according to the location of verlog-eval file. Use the following command to do model inference on EvalMachine. ``` python test_on_nvbench.py --model --n 20 --temperature=0.2 --gpu_name 0 --output_dir --output_file --bench_type Machine ``` If you want to do model inference on EvalHuman, just change the --bench_type from Machine to Human. ``` python test_on_nvbench.py --model --n 20 --temperature=0.2 --gpu_name 0 --output_dir --output_file --bench_type Human ``` Please refer the verilog-eval benchmark repo https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval to evaluate the generated code quality. (3) Test model on RTLLM We provide the organized descriptions of RTLLM as **"rtllm-1.1.json"** in folder **"benchmark_inference"**. Use the following command to do inference on RTLLM benchmark. ``` python test_on_rtllm.py --model --n 5 --temperature=0.5 --gpu_name 0 --output_dir ``` Please refer the RTLLM benchmark repo https://github.com/hkust-zhiyao/RTLLM to evaluate the generated code quality. ## 🏋️ Training We provide three options for instruction tuning: MLE based direct train, Scoring train and Scoring train with gradients splitting. For more details, please refer to the paper and the folder **"train"**. For MLE based direct training, just simply use: ``` torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle.py \ --model_name_or_path \ --data_path \ --fp16 True \ --output_dir \ --num_train_epochs 3 \ --per_device_train_batch_size 2 \ --per_device_eval_batch_size 2 \ --gradient_accumulation_steps 32 \ --evaluation_strategy "no" \ --save_strategy "steps" \ --save_steps 50 \ --save_total_limit 10 \ --learning_rate 1e-5 \ --weight_decay 0. \ --logging_steps 1 \ --tf32 False\ --gradient_checkpointing True \ --deepspeed ds_stage_2.json\ --model_max_length 2048 ``` For scoring based training method, you need to firstly obtain answer candidates to each of the instruction in the training dataset and we provide a data sample **"scoring_data_sample.json"** to illustrate the data format for training. Then use the following command. ``` torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle_scoring.py \ --model_name_or_path \ --data_path \ --fp16 True \ --output_dir \ --num_train_epochs 3 \ --per_device_train_batch_size 1 \ --per_device_eval_batch_size 2 \ --gradient_accumulation_steps 64\ --evaluation_strategy "no" \ --save_strategy "steps" \ --save_steps 50 \ --save_total_limit 10 \ --learning_rate 1e-5 \ --weight_decay 0. \ --logging_steps 1 \ --tf32 False\ --gradient_checkpointing True \ --deepspeed ds_stage_2.json\ --model_max_length 2048 ``` If your gpu could't afford batch size 1 with these answer candidates, try the gradients splitting method. ``` torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle_scoring_grad_split.py \ --model_name_or_path \ --data_path \ --fp16 True \ --output_dir \ --num_train_epochs 3 \ --per_device_train_batch_size 1 \ --per_device_eval_batch_size 2 \ --gradient_accumulation_steps 64\ --evaluation_strategy "no" \ --save_strategy "steps" \ --save_steps 50 \ --save_total_limit 10 \ --learning_rate 1e-5 \ --weight_decay 0. \ --logging_steps 1 \ --tf32 False\ --gradient_checkpointing True \ --deepspeed ds_stage_2.json\ --model_max_length 2048 ``` ## 📝 Citation ``` @article{liu2024rtlcoderb, title={RTLCoder: Fully Open-Source and Efficient LLM-Assisted RTL Code Generation Technique}, author={Liu, Shang and Fang, Wenji and Lu, Yao and Wang, Jing and Zhang, Qijun and Zhang, Hongce and Xie, Zhiyao}, journal={IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems}, year={2024}, publisher={IEEE} } @inproceedings{liu2024rtlcodera, title={RTLCoder: Outperforming GPT-3.5 in Design RTL Generation with Our Open-Source Dataset and Lightweight Solution}, author={Liu, Shang and Fang, Wenji and Lu, Yao and Zhang, Qijun and Zhang, Hongce and Xie, Zhiyao}, booktitle={2024 IEEE International Workshop on LLM-Aided Design}, year={2024}, organization={IEEE} } @inproceedings{liu2024openllm, title={OpenLLM-RTL: Open Dataset and Benchmark for LLM-Aided Design RTL Generation}, author={Liu, Shang and Lu, Yao and Fang, Wenji and Li, Mengming and Xie, Zhiyao}, booktitle={2024 IEEE/ACM International Conference on Computer Aided Design (ICCAD)}, year={2024}, organization={IEEE/ACM} } ```

82	def safe_save_model_for_hf_trainer(trainer: transformers.Trainer, output_dir: str):
83	    """Collects the state dict and dump to disk."""
84	    state_dict = trainer.model.state_dict()
85	    if trainer.args.should_save:
86	        cpu_state_dict = {key: value.cpu() for key, value in state_dict.items()}
87	        del state_dict

91	def smart_tokenizer_and_embedding_resize(
92	    special_tokens_dict: Dict,
93	    tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer,
94	    model: transformers.PreTrainedModel,
95	):
96	    """Resize tokenizer and embedding.
97	
98	    Note: This is the unoptimized version that may make your embedding size not be divisible by 64.
99	    """
100	    num_new_tokens = tokenizer.add_special_tokens(special_tokens_dict)
101	    model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))
102	
103	    if num_new_tokens > 0:
104	        input_embeddings = model.get_input_embeddings().weight.data
105	        output_embeddings = model.get_output_embeddings().weight.data
106	
107	        input_embeddings_avg = input_embeddings[:-num_new_tokens].mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
108	        output_embeddings_avg = output_embeddings[:-num_new_tokens].mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
109	
110	        input_embeddings[-num_new_tokens:] = input_embeddings_avg
111	        output_embeddings[-num_new_tokens:] = output_embeddings_avg
112

116	class ScoreDataset(Dataset):
117	    """Dataset for supervised fine-tuning."""
118	    def __init__(self, data_path: str, tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer, model_max_length=2048):
119	        super(ScoreDataset, self).__init__()
120	        logging.warning("Loading data...")
121	        
122	        with open(data_path, 'r') as f:
123	            lines = f.readlines()
124	        self.dic_temp = [json.loads(line.strip()) for line in lines]
125	        print('total num of data: {}'.format(len(self.dic_temp)))
126	        self.data_temp = [item['Instruction'] for item in self.dic_temp]
127	        self.data = []
128	        index = 0
129	        for data in self.data_temp:
130	            data_toked = _single_tokenize(data, tokenizer)
131	            if data_toked.shape[0] < model_max_length * 0.5:
132	                self.data.append(self.dic_temp[index])
133	            index += 1
134	        print('filtered num of data: {}'.format(len(self.data)))
135	    def __len__(self):
136	        return len(self.data)
137	
138	    def __getitem__(self, i):
139	        return dict(input_ids=self.data[i])
140	
141	def _single_tokenize(text, tokenizer, max_len=None):
142	    if max_len is None:
143	        max_len = tokenizer.model_max_length
144	    toked = tokenizer(
145	            text,
146	            return_tensors="pt",
147	            max_length=max_len,
148	            truncation=True,
149	        )
150	    return toked['input_ids'][0]
151	
152	@dataclass
153	class DataCollatorForSupervisedDataset(object):
154	    """Collate examples for supervised fine-tuning."""
155	
156	    tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer
157	    def __call__(self, instances):
158	
159	        idxs = []
160	        all_scores = []
161	        input_ids = []
162	        score_mask = []
163	        labels = []
164	        for idx, ins in enumerate(instances):
165	
166	            ins = ins['input_ids'] # hack
167	            query = ins['Instruction']
168	            responses = ins['Response']
169	            scores = ins['Score']
170	            all_scores.append(scores)
171	            idxs.append([idx] * len(scores))
172	
173	            query_input_ids = _single_tokenize(query, self.tokenizer, max_len=self.tokenizer.model_max_length)
174	            query_target = torch.LongTensor([IGNORE_INDEX] * (query_input_ids.shape[0]))
175	            dummy_target = torch.LongTensor([IGNORE_INDEX])
176	            for res in responses:
177	                r = res
178	                res_input_ids = _single_tokenize(r + self.tokenizer.eos_token, self.tokenizer, max_len=self.tokenizer.model_max_length-query_input_ids.shape[0]) # eos here
179	                input_ids.append(torch.cat((query_input_ids, res_input_ids), dim=0))
180	                labels.append(torch.cat((query_target, res_input_ids), dim=0))
181	
182	        input_ids = torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence(
183	            input_ids, batch_first=True, padding_value=self.tokenizer.pad_token_id
184	        )
185	        labels = torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence(
186	            labels, batch_first=True, padding_value=IGNORE_INDEX
187	        )
188	
189	        return dict(
190	            input_ids=input_ids,
191	            attention_mask=input_ids.ne(self.tokenizer.pad_token_id),
192	            labels=labels,
193	            idxs=torch.LongTensor(idxs),
194	            scores=torch.FloatTensor(all_scores),
195	        )
196	
197	
198	def make_supervised_data_module(tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer, data_args, training_args) -> Dict:
199	    """Make dataset and collator for supervised fine-tuning."""
200	    train_dataset = ScoreDataset(tokenizer=tokenizer, data_path=data_args.data_path, model_max_length=training_args.model_max_length)
201	    data_collator = DataCollatorForSupervisedDataset(tokenizer=tokenizer)
202	    return dict(train_dataset=train_dataset, eval_dataset=None, data_collator=data_collator)
203	
204	
205	
206	class CompareTrainer(Trainer):
207	
208	    def get_comp_loss(self, logits, labels, attention_mask, scores):
209	        #  this function gather the logits a one batch to calculate the comparing score using normalizing
210	        #logits shape: (batch, can, L, vocab)
211	        #labels shape: (batch, can, L)
212	        #attention_mask shape: (batch, can, L)
213	        #score: (batch, can)
214	        compare_loss_list = []
215	        for batch_id in range(logits.size(0)):
216	            prod = []
217	        #the first dimension of logits should be cand
218	            for i in range(logits.size(1)):
219	                mask = attention_mask[batch_id][i].unsqueeze(0)
220	                logit = logits[batch_id][i].unsqueeze(0)
221	                label = labels[batch_id][i].unsqueeze(0)
222	                loss = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss(reduction="none")(
223	                    logit[..., :-1, :].contiguous().view(-1, logit.size(-1)),
224	                    label[..., 1:].contiguous().view(-1),
225	                ).view(label.size(0), label.size(-1) - 1)
226	                loss = loss * mask[..., 1:].contiguous()
227	                loss = loss[:, -label.size(1):].sum(dim=1)
228	                prod.append(-loss/mask.sum(-1))
229	            prod_tensor = torch.stack(prod)
230	            # prod_normalized = torch.nn.functional.normalize(prod_tensor, p=1, dim=0)
231	            prod_normalized = torch.exp(prod_tensor) / torch.sum(torch.exp(prod_tensor))
232	            # print('**************probability distribution**************')
233	            # print(prod_tensor)
234	            # print('**************normalized**************')
235	            # print(prod_normalized)
236	            comp_loss = self.compare_loss(scores=prod_normalized, rw_scores=scores[batch_id].unsqueeze(0))
237	            compare_loss_list.append(comp_loss)
238	        com_loss_collect = torch.stack(compare_loss_list)
239	        return torch.mean(com_loss_collect)
240	
241	    def compare_loss(self, scores, rw_scores):
242	        # print(scores.shape)  # score shape (batch * cand)
243	        cand = rw_scores.shape[1]
244	        new_scores = scores.reshape(-1, cand)   # batch * cand
245	        diff = new_scores.unsqueeze(1) - new_scores.unsqueeze(-1) # batch * cand * cand
246	        rw_diff = rw_scores.unsqueeze(1) - rw_scores.unsqueeze(-1)
247	        aval = torch.bitwise_and(rw_diff-0.2 > 0, diff-0.3 < 0)
248	        return -(diff[aval]-0.3).sum()
249	
250	    def compute_loss(self, model, inputs, return_outputs=False):
251	        
252	        outputs = model(input_ids=inputs['input_ids'], attention_mask=inputs['attention_mask'])
253	        logits = outputs[0]
254	        batch_size, _ = torch.max(inputs['idxs'][:, 0], dim=0)
255	        batch_size = batch_size.cpu().detach().item() + 1
256	        can_num = int(logits.size(0) / batch_size)
257	        L = logits.size(1)
258	        vocab = logits.size(2) #vocab = 32000
259	        logits = logits.view(batch_size, can_num, L, vocab) # batch * cand * L * V
260	        logits_sft = logits[:,-1,:,:] #batch * L * V
261	        label_len = inputs.get("labels").size(-1)
262	        lable_reshape = inputs.get("labels").view(batch_size, can_num, label_len) # batch * cand * L
263	        lables_sft = lable_reshape[:,-1,:] #batch * L
264	        domain_list = []
265	        for i in range(batch_size):
266	            shift_logits = logits_sft[i,:,:].unsqueeze(0)
267	            shift_logits = shift_logits[..., :-1, :].contiguous()
268	            shift_labels = lables_sft[i,:].unsqueeze(0)
269	            shift_labels = shift_labels[..., 1:].contiguous()
270	            loss_fct = CrossEntropyLoss()
271	            shift_logits = shift_logits.view(-1, vocab)
272	            shift_labels = shift_labels.view(-1)
273	            shift_labels = shift_labels.to(shift_logits.device)
274	            loss_temp = loss_fct(shift_logits, shift_labels)
275	            domain_list.append(loss_temp)
276	        domain_loss = torch.stack(domain_list)
277	        domain_loss = torch.mean(domain_loss)
278	
279	        attention_mask = inputs['attention_mask'].view(batch_size, can_num, L)
280	        scores = inputs['scores'].view(batch_size, can_num)
281	        comp_loss = self.get_comp_loss(logits=logits, labels=lable_reshape, attention_mask=attention_mask, scores=scores)
282	        loss = (self.args.compare_weight * comp_loss + 1) * domain_loss
283	
284	        return (loss, scores) if return_outputs else loss
285	    def training_step(self, model, inputs):
286	        """
287	        Perform a training step on a batch of inputs.
288	
289	        Subclass and override to inject custom behavior.
290	
291	        Args:
292	            model (`nn.Module`):
293	                The model to train.
294	            inputs (`Dict[str, Union[torch.Tensor, Any]]`):
295	                The inputs and targets of the model.
296	
297	                The dictionary will be unpacked before being fed to the model. Most models expect the targets under the
298	                argument `labels`. Check your model's documentation for all accepted arguments.
299	
300	        Return:
301	            `torch.Tensor`: The tensor with training loss on this batch.
302	        """
303	        model.train()
304	        inputs = self._prepare_inputs(inputs)
305	        inputs_1 = copy.deepcopy(inputs)
306	        inputs_2 = copy.deepcopy(inputs)
307	        ##
308	        cand_num = inputs['input_ids'].shape[0]
309	        prod = self.get_sftscore(model=model, inputs=inputs_1)
310	
311	        inputs_score = inputs['scores'][0].reshape(-1, cand_num)
312	        inputs_score = inputs_score.to(device)
313	        prod = prod.to(device)
314	
315	        prod.requires_grad_()
316	
317	        inter_loss = self.args.compare_weight * self.compare_loss(-prod, inputs_score) + prod[torch.argmax(inputs_score[0], dim=0)]
318	        inter_loss.backward()
319	        represent_grad = prod.grad
320	        print_loss = inter_loss
321	
322	        
323	        for i in range(cand_num):
324	            input = {}
325	            input['input_ids'] = inputs_2['input_ids'][i]
326	            input['attention_mask'] = inputs_2['attention_mask'][i]
327	            input['labels'] = inputs_2['labels'][i]
328	            input['idxs'] = inputs_2['idxs'][0][i]
329	            input['scores'] = inputs_2['scores'][0][i]
330	
331	            with self.compute_loss_context_manager():
332	                loss = represent_grad[i] * self.compute_loss(model, input)
333	                # print(loss)
334	            if self.args.n_gpu > 1:
335	                loss = loss.mean()
336	
337	            if self.use_apex:
338	
339	                with amp.scale_loss(loss, self.optimizer) as scaled_loss:
340	                    scaled_loss.backward()
341	            else:
342	                self.accelerator.backward(loss)
343	
344	        return print_loss.detach() / self.args.gradient_accumulation_steps
345	
346	def train():
347	    parser = transformers.HfArgumentParser((ModelArguments, DataArguments, TrainingArguments))
348	    model_args, data_args, training_args = parser.parse_args_into_dataclasses()
349	
350	    model = transformers.AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
351	        model_args.model_name_or_path,
352	        torch_dtype=torch.float16,
353	    )
354	    model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()
355	
356	
357	    tokenizer = transformers.AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(
358	        model_args.model_name_or_path,
359	        cache_dir=training_args.cache_dir,
360	        model_max_length=training_args.model_max_length,
361	        padding_side="left",
362	        use_fast=False,
363	    )
364	    if tokenizer.pad_token is None:
365	        smart_tokenizer_and_embedding_resize(
366	            special_tokens_dict=dict(pad_token=DEFAULT_PAD_TOKEN),
367	            tokenizer=tokenizer,
368	            model=model,
369	        )
370	    if "llama" in model_args.model_name_or_path:
371	        tokenizer.add_special_tokens(
372	            {
373	                "eos_token": DEFAULT_EOS_TOKEN,
374	                "bos_token": DEFAULT_BOS_TOKEN,
375	                "unk_token": DEFAULT_UNK_TOKEN,
376	            }
377	        )
378	
379	    data_module = make_supervised_data_module(tokenizer=tokenizer, data_args=data_args)
380	    trainer = CompareTrainer(model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, args=training_args, **data_module)
381	    trainer.train()
382	    trainer.save_model(output_dir=training_args.output_dir)
383	
384	if __name__ == "__main__":
385	    train()
386

import copy
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Dict, Sequence
import torch
import transformers
from torch.utils.data import Dataset
from transformers import (
    CONFIG_MAPPING,
    MODEL_FOR_CAUSAL_LM_MAPPING,
    AutoConfig,
    AutoModelForCausalLM,
    AutoTokenizer,
    HfArgumentParser,
    Trainer,
    TrainingArguments,
    default_data_collator,
    is_torch_tpu_available,
    set_seed,
    BitsAndBytesConfig
)
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Dict, Sequence
import io
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import transformers
from torch.utils.data import Dataset
from transformers import Trainer
import json
import copy
from accelerate import Accelerator



IGNORE_INDEX = -100
DEFAULT_PAD_TOKEN = "[PAD]"
DEFAULT_EOS_TOKEN = "</s>"
DEFAULT_BOS_TOKEN = "</s>"
DEFAULT_UNK_TOKEN = "</s>"
PROMPT_DICT = {
    "prompt_input": (
        "Below is an instruction that describes a task, paired with an input that provides further context. "
        "Write a response that appropriately completes the request.\n\n"
        "### Instruction:\n{Instruction}\n\n### Input:\n{Input}\n\n### Response:"
    ),
    "prompt_no_input": (
        "Below is an instruction that describes a task. "
        "Write a response that appropriately completes the request.\n\n"
        "### Instruction:\n{Instruction}\n\n### Response:"
    ),
}


@dataclass
class ModelArguments:
    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(default="facebook/opt-125m")


@dataclass
class DataArguments:
    data_path: str = field(default=None, metadata={"help": "Path to the training data."})
    stop_response: bool = field(default=False)


@dataclass
class TrainingArguments(transformers.TrainingArguments):
    cache_dir: Optional[str] = field(default=None)
    optim: str = field(default="adamw_torch")
    model_max_length: int = field(
        default=1024,
        metadata={"help": "Maximum sequence length. Sequences will be right padded (and possibly truncated)."},
    )
    compare_weight: float = field(default=1.0)
    length_penalty: float = field(default=1.0)
    only_use_provide: bool = field(default=False)
    only_use_sample: bool = field(default=False)


def safe_save_model_for_hf_trainer(trainer: transformers.Trainer, output_dir: str):
    """Collects the state dict and dump to disk."""
    state_dict = trainer.model.state_dict()
    if trainer.args.should_save:
        cpu_state_dict = {key: value.cpu() for key, value in state_dict.items()}
        del state_dict
        trainer._save(output_dir, state_dict=cpu_state_dict)  # noqa


def smart_tokenizer_and_embedding_resize(
    special_tokens_dict: Dict,
    tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer,
    model: transformers.PreTrainedModel,
):
    """Resize tokenizer and embedding.

    Note: This is the unoptimized version that may make your embedding size not be divisible by 64.
    """
    num_new_tokens = tokenizer.add_special_tokens(special_tokens_dict)
    model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))

    if num_new_tokens > 0:
        input_embeddings = model.get_input_embeddings().weight.data
        output_embeddings = model.get_output_embeddings().weight.data

        input_embeddings_avg = input_embeddings[:-num_new_tokens].mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
        output_embeddings_avg = output_embeddings[:-num_new_tokens].mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)

        input_embeddings[-num_new_tokens:] = input_embeddings_avg
        output_embeddings[-num_new_tokens:] = output_embeddings_avg




class ScoreDataset(Dataset):
    """Dataset for supervised fine-tuning."""
    def __init__(self, data_path: str, tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer, model_max_length=2048):
        super(ScoreDataset, self).__init__()
        logging.warning("Loading data...")
        
        with open(data_path, 'r') as f:
            lines = f.readlines()
        self.dic_temp = [json.loads(line.strip()) for line in lines]
        print('total num of data: {}'.format(len(self.dic_temp)))
        self.data_temp = [item['Instruction'] for item in self.dic_temp]
        self.data = []
        index = 0
        for data in self.data_temp:
            data_toked = _single_tokenize(data, tokenizer)
            if data_toked.shape[0] < model_max_length * 0.5:
                self.data.append(self.dic_temp[index])
            index += 1
        print('filtered num of data: {}'.format(len(self.data)))
    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

    def __getitem__(self, i):
        return dict(input_ids=self.data[i])

def _single_tokenize(text, tokenizer, max_len=None):
    if max_len is None:
        max_len = tokenizer.model_max_length
    toked = tokenizer(
            text,
            return_tensors="pt",
            max_length=max_len,
            truncation=True,
        )
    return toked['input_ids'][0]

@dataclass
class DataCollatorForSupervisedDataset(object):
    """Collate examples for supervised fine-tuning."""

    tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer
    def __call__(self, instances):

        idxs = []
        all_scores = []
        input_ids = []
        score_mask = []
        labels = []
        for idx, ins in enumerate(instances):

            ins = ins['input_ids'] # hack
            query = ins['Instruction']
            responses = ins['Response']
            scores = ins['Score']
            all_scores.append(scores)
            idxs.append([idx] * len(scores))

            query_input_ids = _single_tokenize(query, self.tokenizer, max_len=self.tokenizer.model_max_length)
            query_target = torch.LongTensor([IGNORE_INDEX] * (query_input_ids.shape[0]))
            dummy_target = torch.LongTensor([IGNORE_INDEX])
            for res in responses:
                r = res
                res_input_ids = _single_tokenize(r + self.tokenizer.eos_token, self.tokenizer, max_len=self.tokenizer.model_max_length-query_input_ids.shape[0]) # eos here
                input_ids.append(torch.cat((query_input_ids, res_input_ids), dim=0))
                labels.append(torch.cat((query_target, res_input_ids), dim=0))

        input_ids = torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence(
            input_ids, batch_first=True, padding_value=self.tokenizer.pad_token_id
        )
        labels = torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence(
            labels, batch_first=True, padding_value=IGNORE_INDEX
        )

        return dict(
            input_ids=input_ids,
            attention_mask=input_ids.ne(self.tokenizer.pad_token_id),
            labels=labels,
            idxs=torch.LongTensor(idxs),
            scores=torch.FloatTensor(all_scores),
        )


def make_supervised_data_module(tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer, data_args, training_args) -> Dict:
    """Make dataset and collator for supervised fine-tuning."""
    train_dataset = ScoreDataset(tokenizer=tokenizer, data_path=data_args.data_path, model_max_length=training_args.model_max_length)
    data_collator = DataCollatorForSupervisedDataset(tokenizer=tokenizer)
    return dict(train_dataset=train_dataset, eval_dataset=None, data_collator=data_collator)



class CompareTrainer(Trainer):

    def get_comp_loss(self, logits, labels, attention_mask, scores):
        #  this function gather the logits a one batch to calculate the comparing score using normalizing
        #logits shape: (batch, can, L, vocab)
        #labels shape: (batch, can, L)
        #attention_mask shape: (batch, can, L)
        #score: (batch, can)
        compare_loss_list = []
        for batch_id in range(logits.size(0)):
            prod = []
        #the first dimension of logits should be cand
            for i in range(logits.size(1)):
                mask = attention_mask[batch_id][i].unsqueeze(0)
                logit = logits[batch_id][i].unsqueeze(0)
                label = labels[batch_id][i].unsqueeze(0)
                loss = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss(reduction="none")(
                    logit[..., :-1, :].contiguous().view(-1, logit.size(-1)),
                    label[..., 1:].contiguous().view(-1),
                ).view(label.size(0), label.size(-1) - 1)
                loss = loss * mask[..., 1:].contiguous()
                loss = loss[:, -label.size(1):].sum(dim=1)
                prod.append(-loss/mask.sum(-1))
            prod_tensor = torch.stack(prod)
            # prod_normalized = torch.nn.functional.normalize(prod_tensor, p=1, dim=0)
            prod_normalized = torch.exp(prod_tensor) / torch.sum(torch.exp(prod_tensor))
            # print('**************probability distribution**************')
            # print(prod_tensor)
            # print('**************normalized**************')
            # print(prod_normalized)
            comp_loss = self.compare_loss(scores=prod_normalized, rw_scores=scores[batch_id].unsqueeze(0))
            compare_loss_list.append(comp_loss)
        com_loss_collect = torch.stack(compare_loss_list)
        return torch.mean(com_loss_collect)

    def compare_loss(self, scores, rw_scores):
        # print(scores.shape)  # score shape (batch * cand)
        cand = rw_scores.shape[1]
        new_scores = scores.reshape(-1, cand)   # batch * cand
        diff = new_scores.unsqueeze(1) - new_scores.unsqueeze(-1) # batch * cand * cand
        rw_diff = rw_scores.unsqueeze(1) - rw_scores.unsqueeze(-1)
        aval = torch.bitwise_and(rw_diff-0.2 > 0, diff-0.3 < 0)
        return -(diff[aval]-0.3).sum()

    def compute_loss(self, model, inputs, return_outputs=False):
        
        outputs = model(input_ids=inputs['input_ids'], attention_mask=inputs['attention_mask'])
        logits = outputs[0]
        batch_size, _ = torch.max(inputs['idxs'][:, 0], dim=0)
        batch_size = batch_size.cpu().detach().item() + 1
        can_num = int(logits.size(0) / batch_size)
        L = logits.size(1)
        vocab = logits.size(2) #vocab = 32000
        logits = logits.view(batch_size, can_num, L, vocab) # batch * cand * L * V
        logits_sft = logits[:,-1,:,:] #batch * L * V
        label_len = inputs.get("labels").size(-1)
        lable_reshape = inputs.get("labels").view(batch_size, can_num, label_len) # batch * cand * L
        lables_sft = lable_reshape[:,-1,:] #batch * L
        domain_list = []
        for i in range(batch_size):
            shift_logits = logits_sft[i,:,:].unsqueeze(0)
            shift_logits = shift_logits[..., :-1, :].contiguous()
            shift_labels = lables_sft[i,:].unsqueeze(0)
            shift_labels = shift_labels[..., 1:].contiguous()
            loss_fct = CrossEntropyLoss()
            shift_logits = shift_logits.view(-1, vocab)
            shift_labels = shift_labels.view(-1)
            shift_labels = shift_labels.to(shift_logits.device)
            loss_temp = loss_fct(shift_logits, shift_labels)
            domain_list.append(loss_temp)
        domain_loss = torch.stack(domain_list)
        domain_loss = torch.mean(domain_loss)

        attention_mask = inputs['attention_mask'].view(batch_size, can_num, L)
        scores = inputs['scores'].view(batch_size, can_num)
        comp_loss = self.get_comp_loss(logits=logits, labels=lable_reshape, attention_mask=attention_mask, scores=scores)
        loss = (self.args.compare_weight * comp_loss + 1) * domain_loss

        return (loss, scores) if return_outputs else loss
    def training_step(self, model, inputs):
        """
        Perform a training step on a batch of inputs.

        Subclass and override to inject custom behavior.

        Args:
            model (`nn.Module`):
                The model to train.
            inputs (`Dict[str, Union[torch.Tensor, Any]]`):
                The inputs and targets of the model.

                The dictionary will be unpacked before being fed to the model. Most models expect the targets under the
                argument `labels`. Check your model's documentation for all accepted arguments.

        Return:
            `torch.Tensor`: The tensor with training loss on this batch.
        """
        model.train()
        inputs = self._prepare_inputs(inputs)
        inputs_1 = copy.deepcopy(inputs)
        inputs_2 = copy.deepcopy(inputs)
        ##
        cand_num = inputs['input_ids'].shape[0]
        prod = self.get_sftscore(model=model, inputs=inputs_1)

        inputs_score = inputs['scores'][0].reshape(-1, cand_num)
        inputs_score = inputs_score.to(device)
        prod = prod.to(device)

        prod.requires_grad_()

        inter_loss = self.args.compare_weight * self.compare_loss(-prod, inputs_score) + prod[torch.argmax(inputs_score[0], dim=0)]
        inter_loss.backward()
        represent_grad = prod.grad
        print_loss = inter_loss

        
        for i in range(cand_num):
            input = {}
            input['input_ids'] = inputs_2['input_ids'][i]
            input['attention_mask'] = inputs_2['attention_mask'][i]
            input['labels'] = inputs_2['labels'][i]
            input['idxs'] = inputs_2['idxs'][0][i]
            input['scores'] = inputs_2['scores'][0][i]

            with self.compute_loss_context_manager():
                loss = represent_grad[i] * self.compute_loss(model, input)
                # print(loss)
            if self.args.n_gpu > 1:
                loss = loss.mean()

            if self.use_apex:

                with amp.scale_loss(loss, self.optimizer) as scaled_loss:
                    scaled_loss.backward()
            else:
                self.accelerator.backward(loss)

        return print_loss.detach() / self.args.gradient_accumulation_steps

def train():
    parser = transformers.HfArgumentParser((ModelArguments, DataArguments, TrainingArguments))
    model_args, data_args, training_args = parser.parse_args_into_dataclasses()

    model = transformers.AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_args.model_name_or_path,
        torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    )
    model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()


    tokenizer = transformers.AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(
        model_args.model_name_or_path,
        cache_dir=training_args.cache_dir,
        model_max_length=training_args.model_max_length,
        padding_side="left",
        use_fast=False,
    )
    if tokenizer.pad_token is None:
        smart_tokenizer_and_embedding_resize(
            special_tokens_dict=dict(pad_token=DEFAULT_PAD_TOKEN),
            tokenizer=tokenizer,
            model=model,
        )
    if "llama" in model_args.model_name_or_path:
        tokenizer.add_special_tokens(
            {
                "eos_token": DEFAULT_EOS_TOKEN,
                "bos_token": DEFAULT_BOS_TOKEN,
                "unk_token": DEFAULT_UNK_TOKEN,
            }
        )

    data_module = make_supervised_data_module(tokenizer=tokenizer, data_args=data_args)
    trainer = CompareTrainer(model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, args=training_args, **data_module)
    trainer.train()
    trainer.save_model(output_dir=training_args.output_dir)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    train()

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the three training schemes. Let me note the key facts:

## Three training schemes:

### 1. MLE based direct training (`mle.py`)
- Standard SFT
- `SupervisedDataset`: takes `Response[-1]` as the target
- `preprocess`: tokenize `source + target`, then mask out the source part with `IGNORE_INDEX = -100`
- `DataCollatorForSupervisedDataset`: pads input_ids (pad_token_id) and labels (IGNORE_INDEX), builds attention_mask
- README command: `torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle.py ... --per_device_train_batch_size 2 --gradient_accumulation_steps 32 --model_max_length 2048`
- Uses base `Trainer`
- `model_max_length` default 2048
- Only consumes `{Instruction, Response}` (from Resyn27k.json which has no Input)

### 2. Scoring training (`mle_scoring.py`)
- Uses `CompareTrainer(Trainer)` which overrides `compute_loss`
- `ScoreDataset`: filters candidates by length - `data_toked.shape[0] < model_max_length * 0.5` (only keeps instructions whose tokenized length < half of model_max_length, so there's room for multiple candidates)
- `DataCollatorForSupervisedDataset` (different from mle.py's!): for each instance, takes query (Instruction), and loops over all responses, building one sequence `query + response_i` per candidate. Stacks them all into a flat batch with `idxs` (which candidate belongs to which batch row) and `scores`.
- The key tensors returned: `input_ids` (batch*cand, L), `labels`, `attention_mask`, `idxs` (batch*cand, cand), `scores` (batch, cand)
- README command: `torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle_scoring.py ... --per_device_train_batch_size 1 --gradient_accumulation_steps 64 --model_max_length 2048`
- `model_max_length` default 1024
- Consumes `{Instruction, Input, Response[N], Score[N]}`

### 3. Scoring training with gradient splitting (`mle_scoring_grad_split.py`)
- Same `CompareTrainer` but ALSO overrides `training_step`
- The `training_step` splits the forward/backward into: first compute a "representational gradient" `represent_grad` via a SFT-score forward, then loop over candidates one-by-one, weighting each candidate's `compute_loss` by `represent_grad[i]`
- This reduces peak memory because you don't hold activations for ALL candidates simultaneously — you do them one at a time
- README: "If your gpu could't afford batch size 1 with these answer candidates, try the gradients splitting method."
- Same command params as mle_scoring.py

Now, about the `model_max_length * 0.5` filter — note this is the key: it filters on the **Instruction** tokenized length being less than half of model_max_length, so that query + a few responses can fit. Actually wait, let me re-read.

```python
for data in self.data_temp:
    data_toked = _single_tokenize(data, tokenizer)
    if data_toked.shape[0] < model_max_length * 0.5:
        self.data.append(self.dic_temp[index])
    index += 1
```

`self.data_temp = [item['Instruction'] for item in self.dic_temp]`

So it tokenizes just the `Instruction` text and checks if its length is less than `model_max_length * 0.5`. So if model_max_length=2048, the instruction must be < 1024 tokens. This is to ensure there's enough room to concatenate query + multiple candidate responses. The rationale spec calls it "候选长度过滤（model_max_length*0.5）" which translates to "candidate length filter (model_max_length*0.5)". More precisely it filters the instruction length.

Let me make sure I describe this accurately: it filters out examples whose Instruction is too long (≥ half of model_max_length), to leave room for concatenating query + multiple responses within model_max_length.

Now, the `DataCollator` in mle_scoring.py — the key insight about "多候选 query+response 拼接":
- For each instance (one Instruction), there's one `query` and N `responses` (and N scores)
- For each response, it builds `input_ids = cat(query_input_ids, res_input_ids)` and `labels = cat(query_target [all IGNORE], res_input_ids)`
- So one instance produces N sequences (one per candidate)
- The batch is the flat list of ALL these sequences across all instances in the batch
- `idxs` tracks which instance each sequence belongs to: `idxs.append([idx] * len(scores))` where `idx` is the instance index in the batch
- `all_scores` collects the score list per instance

So for per_device_train_batch_size=1, there's 1 instance, but if it has 4 candidates, the actual sequence count passed to model is 4. The model sees a batch of 4 sequences, then compute_loss reshapes (batch_size=1, can_num=4, L, vocab).

Now the `compute_loss` reshape logic:
```python
batch_size, _ = torch.max(inputs['idxs'][:, 0], dim=0)
batch_size = batch_size.cpu().detach().item() + 1
can_num = int(logits.size(0) / batch_size)
```
So it recovers batch_size from the max idx value, and can_num = total_sequences / batch_size.

OK, I have a thorough understanding. Let me also verify the README training section details for the comparison table practice task. The three commands differ:
- mle.py: `--per_device_train_batch_size 2 --gradient_accumulation_steps 32`
- mle_scoring.py: `--per_device_train_batch_size 1 --gradient_accumulation_steps 64`
- mle_scoring_grad_split.py: `--per_device_train_batch_size 1 --gradient_accumulation_steps 64`

All use `--nproc_per_node=4`, `--fp16 True`, `--num_train_epochs 3`, `--learning_rate 1e-5`, `--gradient_checkpointing True`, `--deepspeed ds_stage_2.json`, `--model_max_length 2048`.

Now I'll note the design intent clearly: the scoring method needs per_device_train_batch_size=1 (because each "1" instance is multiplied into N candidate sequences, exploding memory), and double the gradient_accumulation_steps (64 vs 32) to compensate for the smaller effective batch. The grad_split method is the escape hatch when even batch=1 doesn't fit.

# 训练方案总览与共享数据管线

Let me think about each minimal module's content carefully.

### 4.1 Three training schemes

The README says (line 236): "We provide three options for instruction tuning: MLE based direct train, Scoring train and Scoring train with gradients splitting."

- **MLE based direct train** (`mle.py`): Standard supervised fine-tuning. One instruction → one reference answer. The model learns to imitate the reference code. Cheapest, the baseline.
- **Scoring train** (`mle_scoring.py`): The paper's core contribution. One instruction → multiple candidate answers, each with a quality score. The model learns not just to imitate but to *prefer* higher-quality candidates (comparison loss) while still doing SFT on the best candidate (domain loss). Better accuracy but more memory (each example is multiplied by N candidates).
- **Scoring train with gradient splitting** (`mle_scoring_grad_split.py`): Same learning objective as scoring train, but rewrites `training_step` to split the forward pass candidate-by-candidate, so you can fit scoring training on smaller GPUs. Used "if your gpu couldn't afford batch size 1 with these answer candidates."

The relationship: mle_scoring_grad_split.py is essentially mle_scoring.py + a custom training_step. They share the same ScoreDataset, DataCollator, CompareTrainer.compute_loss. The only addition is the overridden training_step.

When to use each:
- mle.py: baseline, if you only have {Instruction, Response} data (like Resyn27k.json), or limited compute
- mle_scoring.py: if you have scored candidate data AND enough GPU memory to hold all candidates of one example at once
- mle_scoring_grad_split.py: if you have scored candidate data but GPU memory is too tight for mle_scoring.py

The three torchrun commands all share: `--nproc_per_node=4 --fp16 True --num_train_epochs 3 --learning_rate 1e-5 --gradient_checkpointing True --deepspeed ds_stage_2.json --model_max_length 2048`. They differ in:
- mle.py: `--per_device_train_batch_size 2 --gradient_accumulation_steps 32`
- mle_scoring.py & grad_split: `--per_device_train_batch_size 1 --gradient_accumulation_steps 64`

Why batch=1 for scoring? Because each "example" expands into N candidate sequences in the collator, so the real per-step sequence count = per_device_train_batch_size × N. With N≈4, batch=2 would mean 8 sequences' activations held simultaneously → OOM. So batch is forced to 1, and gradient_accumulation_steps is doubled (64 vs 32) to keep the effective batch size similar (effective = nproc × batch × grad_accum = 4×1×64 = 256 for scoring vs 4×2×32 = 256 for mle). Nice — effective batch is the same 256.

That's a great insight for the comparison table practice.

### 4.2 ScoreDataset length filter

The filter: `if data_toked.shape[0] < model_max_length * 0.5`. It tokenizes ONLY the Instruction (the query). If the query alone is ≥ half of model_max_length, the example is dropped. Why half? Because in the collator, the final sequence is `query + response_i`, and there are N such sequences. To leave room for responses (and to keep candidate sequences from being truncated), the query is capped at half the max length. 

Wait — but actually each candidate sequence is `query + one response`, and that single sequence is capped at model_max_length via truncation in `_single_tokenize` (`max_len=self.tokenizer.model_max_length-query_input_ids.shape[0]`). So a single sequence can't exceed model_max_length anyway. The half-filter is a heuristic to ensure the query isn't so long that responses get truncated to near-nothing, and more importantly it's a proxy for "this example is short enough to be useful for multi-candidate training."

I should describe this accurately: it filters on the Instruction (query) length being < half of model_max_length. This is a pre-filter at dataset construction time. The print statements show "total num of data" vs "filtered num of data".

Also note: ScoreDataset.__getitem__ returns `dict(input_ids=self.data[i])` — note `self.data[i]` is the WHOLE original dict (with Instruction, Input, Response, Score), NOT tokenized ids. The field name `input_ids` is misleading — it's a "hack" (the collator does `ins = ins['input_ids']` then accesses `ins['Instruction']` etc.). This is an important quirk to point out.

Default model_max_length in mle_scoring.py TrainingArguments is 1024 (lines 49-52), but README commands pass `--model_max_length 2048`, so the filter is `Instruction tokens < 1024`.

Note mle.py's SupervisedDataset has NO such filter — it just loads everything and tokenizes source+target.

### 4.3 DataCollator multi-candidate concatenation

This is the heart of the shared pipeline. Let me trace it:

```python
for idx, ins in enumerate(instances):
    ins = ins['input_ids']  # hack: it's actually the whole dict
    query = ins['Instruction']
    responses = ins['Response']   # list of N candidates
    scores = ins['Score']         # list of N scores
    all_scores.append(scores)
    idxs.append([idx] * len(scores))  # tag each candidate with its instance index

    query_input_ids = _single_tokenize(query, ...)
    query_target = torch.LongTensor([IGNORE_INDEX] * query_len)  # mask the query
    for res in responses:
        res_input_ids = _single_tokenize(res + eos_token, ..., max_len=model_max_length - query_len)
        input_ids.append(cat(query_input_ids, res_input_ids))   # query+response
        labels.append(cat(query_target, res_input_ids))          # query masked, response as target
```

So one instance with N candidates produces N (input_ids, labels) pairs. The whole batch is the flat concatenation. Then:
- `input_ids` padded to (total_seq, L) with pad_token_id
- `labels` padded with IGNORE_INDEX
- `attention_mask = input_ids != pad`
- `idxs`: shape (total_seq, cand_per_instance) — each row is `[idx]*N`, tagging membership
- `scores`: shape (batch_instances, N)

Key point: the model is called with `total_seq = batch_instances × N` sequences. compute_loss then reshapes logits from (total_seq, L, vocab) back to (batch_instances, N, L, vocab) using `can_num = total_seq / batch_size`.

This is the "多候选 query+response 拼接" — one logical example becomes N physical sequences, all sharing the same query tokens (re-tokenized N times, not cached — a minor inefficiency worth noting).

Comparison with mle.py's collator: mle.py's DataCollator gets one (input_ids, labels) per instance and pads them. Simple. The scoring collator expands one instance into N.

I should emphasize the contrast:
- mle.py: dataset returns pre-tokenized (input_ids, labels); collator just pads. Target = Response[-1] (the single reference).
- mle_scoring.py: dataset returns the raw dict; collator does ALL the tokenization + candidate expansion + label masking on the fly.

This is a clean structural contrast.

Now the practice task for this module: I'll have the reader trace through one scoring_data_sample.json line (e.g., the multiplier example with 4 candidates and scores [0.477, 0.489, 0.433, 1]) and compute by hand what tensors the collator would produce: 4 sequences, idxs = [[0,0,0,0]], scores = [[0.477, 0.489, 0.433, 1]], etc. Note the last candidate has Score=1 (the reference).

Now let me write the lecture. I need to be careful with:
- Math formulas use \( ... \) and \[ ... \], not $...$
- Permalinks: format `[文件路径:L起始-L结束](<permalink_base>/<相对路径>#L起始-L结束)`
- Chinese throughout
- Don't fabricate
- Mark "待本地验证" where I can't confirm runtime results

Let me also double check the mle.py target: `targets = [f"{example['Response'][-1]}{tokenizer.eos_token}" ...]`. Yes, Response[-1]. In Resyn27k.json, Response is a single-element list (per u1-l4), so Response[-1] is that one reference. Good.

One more thing about the mle.py `preprocess` — the label masking. It tokenizes `source+target` together as `examples`, and `source` alone. Then sets `label[:source_len] = IGNORE_INDEX`. This masks the instruction part. But note a subtle bug/feature: `source_len` is computed as `input_ids.ne(pad_token_id).sum()` which counts non-pad tokens, and since there's no padding yet (padding="longest" within _tokenize_fn but it's called per-string), it's basically the source length. Actually `input_ids_lens = tokenized.input_ids.ne(tokenizer.pad_token_id).sum().item()`. Since each string is tokenized individually with padding="longest" (which is just itself for a single string), this is just the length. OK. This is u2-l7's territory, I'll just mention briefly that mle.py masks the instruction with IGNORE_INDEX and leave deep detail to u2-l7.

Actually, since u2-l7 is "mle.py 标准监督微调" which goes deep into preprocess/IGNORE_INDEX, I should keep this lecture's treatment of mle.py's internals light and point forward to u2-l7. This lecture is the OVERVIEW.

Let me write it now. I'll aim for clear, beginner-friendly, with tables and pseudocode.

For permalinks, the base is:

`[train/mle.py:114-139](https://github.com/hkust-zhiyao/RTL-Coder/blob/b2847073be62d5f1d6d9b17bb247f0cfeb1ce642/train/mle.py#L114-L139)`

The format in the spec is `[文件路径:L起始-L结束](<permalink_base>/<相对路径>#L起始-L结束)`. So I'll write it as the display text being `train/mle.py:114-139` and the URL being the full permalink. Let me use that format.

Let me write the full lecture now.

For the comparison table practice, I'll provide the answer table myself as part of the practice solution since the practice is "列一张表对比三种方案...". Actually the practice_task says: "阅读 README 的训练章节，列一张表对比三种方案的 torchrun 命令、per_device_train_batch_size、gradient_accumulation_steps 与显存需求差异。" 

So in the 代码实践 section, I'll lay out the steps for the reader to build this table, and I can give a reference answer table (since the data is all in the README which I've read). This is a "源码阅读型实践" — reading the README and extracting the comparison.

| 方案 | 脚本 | torchrun 入口 | per_device_train_batch_size | gradient_accumulation_steps | 有效 batch (4卡) | 显存需求 |

| MLE 直接训练 | mle.py | `torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle.py` | 2 | 32 | 4×2×32=256 | 最低 |
| 评分训练 | mle_scoring.py | `torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle_scoring.py` | 1 | 64 | 4×1×64=256 | 高（每例×N候选） |
| 评分训练+梯度切分 | mle_scoring_grad_split.py | `torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 mle_scoring_grad_split.py` | 1 | 64 | 4×1×64=256 | 中（算法层降低峰值） |

Good. All three have effective batch 256 (nice observation). The key difference is per_device_train_batch_size and the memory.

Now writing. Let me make sure about the "显存需求" — README says grad_split is for "If your gpu could't afford batch size 1 with these answer candidates" — so grad_split < scoring in memory. And mle.py is the cheapest. So: mle < grad_split < scoring in memory usage. Wait, let me think — grad_split achieves LOWER peak memory than scoring (that's its whole point), so it can run where scoring can't. So memory ranking (peak): mle.py lowest, then grad_split, then scoring highest. Yes.

Let me write the lecture. I'll keep code snippets short.

Actually, let me reconsider the structure. The standard structure requires each minimal module (4.x) to have 4.x.1 概念说明, 4.x.2 核心流程, 4.x.3 源码精读, 4.x.4 代码实践, 4.x.5 小练习与答案. I'll follow this for the 3 minimal modules. That's a lot but appropriate.

Let me be efficient and write tight content. Let me draft.

For module 4.3 source reading, I want to show the collator code. Let me grab the key lines 136-152 (the loop) and 161-167 (return).

Let me write it all now.

One note on the `idxs` semantics for the practice. In the multiplier example:
- 1 instance (per_device_train_batch_size=1)
- 4 candidates
- `idxs = [[0]*4]` = `[[0,0,0,0]]`, shape (4, 4)
- `all_scores = [[0.477, 0.489, 0.433, 1]]`, → scores shape (1, 4)
- `input_ids` shape (4, L), `labels` shape (4, L)
- In compute_loss: `batch_size = max(idxs[:,0])+1 = 0+1 = 1`, `can_num = 4/1 = 4`. Good.

So idxs has shape (total_seq, cand) = (4, 4) here. Each of the 4 rows is [0,0,0,0]. The collator builds `idxs.append([idx] * len(scores))` per instance, so each instance contributes `len(scores)` rows each equal to `[idx]*len(scores)`. So idxs is (total_seq, N) where each row repeats the instance idx N times. It's a bit redundant but compute_loss only reads `idxs[:,0]` (the first column) via `torch.max(inputs['idxs'][:, 0], dim=0)`. So effectively only the first column matters. Good detail — shows why scoring needs training_args at dataset-build time. I'll mention it.

Also mle.py default model_max_length=2048, scoring default=1024 (but README overrides to 2048). Note this.

@dataclass
class DataCollatorForSupervisedDataset(object):
    """Collate examples for supervised fine-tuning."""

    tokenizer: transformers.PreTrainedTokenizer
    def __call__(self, instances):

        idxs = []
        all_scores = []
        input_ids = []
        score_mask = []
        labels = []
        for idx, ins in enumerate(instances):

            ins = ins['input_ids'] # hack
            query = ins['Instruction']
            responses = ins['Response']
            scores = ins['Score']
            all_scores.append(scores)
            idxs.append([idx] * len(scores))

            query_input_ids = _single_tokenize(query, self.tokenizer, max_len=self.tokenizer.model_max_length)
            query_target = torch.LongTensor([IGNORE_INDEX] * (query_input_ids.shape[0]))
            dummy_target = torch.LongTensor([IGNORE_INDEX])
            for res in responses:
                r = res
                res_input_ids = _single_tokenize(r + self.tokenizer.eos_token, self.tokenizer, max_len=self.tokenizer.model_max_length-query_input_ids.shape[0]) # eos here
                input_ids.append(torch.cat((query_input_ids, res_input_ids), dim=0))
                labels.append(torch.cat((query_target, res_input_ids), dim=0))

        input_ids = torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence(
            input_ids, batch_first=True, padding_value=self.tokenizer.pad_token_id
        )
        labels = torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence(
            labels, batch_first=True, padding_value=IGNORE_INDEX
        )

        return dict(
            input_ids=input_ids,
            attention_mask=input_ids.ne(self.tokenizer.pad_token_id),
            labels=labels,
            idxs=torch.LongTensor(idxs),
            scores=torch.FloatTensor(all_scores),
        )
